matter.title: Formation of Summit Outfitters Co. and S-Corporation Election by Grace C. Roe

jurisdiction.court_type: Maine Secretary of State / IRS (filing offices)

matter.summary: Grace C. Roe is forming Summit Outfitters Co., a Maine corporation engaged in software development, and electing S-corporation status effective 2025-12-19; the engagement covers the Articles of Incorporation, the EIN application (Form SS-4), the timely S-election (Form 2553), and Maine corporate and payroll-withholding registration.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe is forming Summit Outfitters Co., a Maine corporation engaged in software development, and electing S-corporation status effective 2025-12-19; the engagement covers the Articles of Incorporation, the EIN application (Form SS-4), the timely S-election (Form 2553), and Maine corporate and payroll-withholding registration.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe is the founder and IRS responsible party for Summit Outfitters Co., a new corporation being organized in Maine to carry on software development. Articles of Incorporation were filed with the Maine Secretary of State with an incorporation date of 2025-12-19, and the corporation will have 2 shareholder(s) at formation.

The founder intends to elect S-corporation treatment under Subchapter S so that the corporation's income passes through to the shareholders, electing on a calendar year basis with a fiscal year end of December 31 and effective on 2025-12-19. The corporation expects 5 employee(s) and plans to begin payroll on or about 2026-01-27, using the cash method of accounting and reporting under NAICS code 453998.

Because S-corporation owner-employees must be paid reasonable wages, the founder plans owner compensation of approximately $61995 per year and projects first-year revenue near $729078. The remaining work is to obtain an EIN on Form SS-4, file Form 2553 within the statutory window so the election is timely, register with Maine Revenue Services for corporate income tax and employer withholding, and set up payroll so the first Maine wage filings are correct.
undisputed_facts: Summit Outfitters Co. is being organized as a corporation in Maine with an incorporation date of 2025-12-19.
Grace C. Roe is the founder and the IRS responsible party for the corporation.
The corporation intends to elect S-corporation status effective 2025-12-19.
The corporation expects 2 shareholder(s) and approximately 5 employee(s) in its first year.
disputed_facts: Whether owner compensation of approximately $61995 satisfies the reasonable-compensation standard for an S-corporation owner-employee given the activity and revenue.
Whether the Form 2553 election will be filed within the statutory window so that S status is effective on 2025-12-19 without needing late-election relief.
Whether the cash method of accounting is the appropriate choice for the corporation's first tax year.

timeline.event: EIN application (Form SS-4) and S-election (Form 2553) prepared for filing with the IRS for Summit Outfitters Co..
Articles of Incorporation for Summit Outfitters Co. filed with the Maine Secretary of State.
Corporation begins payroll for its owner-employee and any staff, triggering Maine withholding obligations.

third_parties.name: Internal Revenue Service
third_parties.relevance: Issues the EIN on Form SS-4 and processes the Form 2553 S-corporation election.

third_parties.name: Casco Bay Savings
third_parties.relevance: Holds the corporation's operating account, which is opened using the new EIN once issued.

third_parties.name: Rosa T. Birchwood
third_parties.relevance: Advises on the S-election, reasonable compensation, payroll setup, and prepares the corporation's federal and Maine tax filings.

third_parties.name: Maria T. Coe
third_parties.relevance: A second shareholder whose eligibility affects the S-corporation election and who must consent on Form 2553.

exchanges.question: Tell me about the business and why you want a corporation with an S-election.
exchanges.answer: I'm starting Summit Outfitters Co. to do software development. I want the liability protection of a corporation, but I'd rather have the profits pass through to me instead of being taxed at the corporate level, so an S-election makes sense.

exchanges.question: Who owns the company, and is everyone eligible to hold S-corporation stock?
exchanges.answer: There are 2 of us on the cap table. We're all individuals and U.S. persons, so I believe we all qualify, and everyone is on board with making the S-election.

exchanges.question: Will the company have employees, and what are your payroll plans?
exchanges.answer: We expect about 5 employee(s) to start, and I plan to put myself on payroll as an owner-employee. I'd like to run the first payroll around 2026-01-27, and I want to make sure we withhold Maine taxes correctly.

exchanges.question: Where do things stand on the EIN and the business bank account?
exchanges.answer: I still need the EIN before I can open the company's bank account, so getting Form SS-4 done is my first priority. Once the EIN comes through I'll open the operating account at the business bank.

exchanges.question: What does a successful setup look like for you?
exchanges.answer: Limited liability, pass-through taxation with a reasonable salary for myself, a clean EIN and payroll setup, and being fully registered in Maine. Above all, I cannot afford to miss the S-election deadline.

goals: Form Summit Outfitters Co. as a Maine corporation with limited liability for its shareholders.
Make a timely and effective S-corporation election so income passes through to the shareholders.
Obtain the EIN and set up compliant federal and Maine payroll, including owner reasonable compensation.
Complete Maine registration for corporate income tax and employer withholding.
priorities: Filing Form 2553 within the statutory window so the S-election is effective on 2025-12-19.
Paying the owner-employee reasonable compensation that withstands IRS scrutiny.
Proper EIN, payroll, and Maine withholding setup from the first payroll.
desired_outcomes: An approved Maine corporation with an effective S-election as of 2025-12-19.
An issued EIN, a funded operating account, and correct first-year federal and Maine payroll filings.
non_starters: Blowing the S-election deadline and losing pass-through treatment for the first year.

constraints.budget: Limited; prefers to handle routine filings in-house with counsel reviewing the elections.
constraints.timeline: Needs the EIN and Form 2553 filed in time for the election to be effective on the incorporation date.

issues.issue: Pass-through treatment and owner fringe-benefit rules
sub_questions: How do items of income, loss, and deduction pass through to the shareholders?
Which fringe benefits are limited for more-than-2% shareholder-employees?
issues.client_position: The founder seeks full pass-through treatment and to structure any owner fringe benefits in light of the 2% shareholder-employee rules.

issues.issue: Eligibility of the corporation and its shareholders to elect S status
sub_questions: Are all shareholders eligible (individuals, estates, or qualifying trusts) and within the 100-shareholder limit?
Does the corporation have only one class of stock as required?
issues.client_position: The corporation has eligible individual shareholders and a single class of stock, so it qualifies as a small business corporation.

issues.issue: Reasonable compensation for the S-corporation owner-employee
issues.governing_law: Reasonable compensation doctrine (IRS guidance; Rev. Rul. 74-44)
sub_questions: Is the planned $61995 owner salary reasonable for the services performed?
How should wages and distributions be allocated to withstand reclassification?
issues.client_position: Planned owner compensation of approximately $61995 reflects the value of services and is intended to satisfy the reasonable-compensation standard.

expert_opinions.topic: Valuation support for owner compensation and initial capitalization
expert_opinions.opinion: The planned owner salary falls within a defensible range for the services rendered and the corporation's projected operations.
expert_opinions.basis: Market compensation data, the business plan, and the initial capitalization.

evidence.title: Initial capitalization / cap table
evidence.description: The cap table showing the 2 shareholder(s) and their initial capital contributions.

evidence.title: Articles of Incorporation filed with the Maine Secretary of State
evidence.description: The filed Articles establishing Summit Outfitters Co. as a Maine corporation.

evidence.title: IRS Form SS-4 application
evidence.description: The Application for Employer Identification Number identifying Grace C. Roe as the responsible party.

amounts.label: Expected first-year revenue
amounts.basis: Projected gross receipts for the corporation's first tax year.

amounts.label: Estimated first-year payroll
amounts.basis: Total projected wages for all employees in the first year.

amounts.label: Initial capital contributed
amounts.basis: Total cash and property contributed by the shareholders at formation.
